docs.title: Python Development Playbook
docs.text: Welcome to our Python Development Playbook! Having a standardized playbook helps maintain consistency and quality across projects while reducing setup time. These essential tools and practices will help you write better, more maintainable Python code while ensuring everyone follows the same proven standards.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to use GitHub repository templates to bootstrap your projects
  • Understanding modern Python project configuration with pyproject.toml
  • Fast and efficient package management with uv
  • Code quality and linting with ruff
  • Documentation with MkDocs and GitHub Pages
  • Testing best practices with pytest
  • Automating your workflow with GitHub Actions

Let's dive in and explore these tools and practices that will make your Python development experience more efficient and enjoyable!

docs.title: What are GitHub Actions?
docs.text: GitHub Actions is a continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) platform that allows you to automate your build, test, and deployment pipeline. It's integrated directly into GitHub and allows you to create custom workflows for your projects.

docs.title: Why Use GitHub Actions?
docs.text:
• Integrated: Native GitHub integration
  • Flexible: Customizable workflows
  • Powerful: Extensive marketplace of actions
  • Free: Generous free tier for public repositories
  • Secure: Built-in secrets management

docs.title: Where are Actions Executed?
docs.text:
GitHub Actions run on GitHub-hosted runners or self-hosted runners:

  • GitHub-hosted runners:
  • Ubuntu Linux
  • Windows
  • macOS

  • Different hardware specifications available

  • Self-hosted runners:

  • Run on your own infrastructure
  • Custom hardware
  • Custom operating systems
  • Private network access

docs.title: When are Actions Executed?
docs.text:
Actions can be triggered by various events:

  • Push events: When code is pushed to a branch
  • Pull request events: When a PR is created, updated, or merged
  • Schedule: Using cron syntax
  • Manual: Using workflow_dispatch
  • Repository events: Issues, releases, etc.
  • External events: Using repository_dispatch

docs.title: Basic Workflow Example
docs.text:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
  pull_request:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v3
    - name: Set up Python
      uses: actions/setup-python@v4
      with:
        python-version: '3.x'
    - name: Install dependencies
      run: |
        python -m pip install --upgrade pip
        pip install -r requirements.txt
    - name: Run tests
      run: |
        pytest

docs.title: 1. Python Testing
docs.text:
name: Python Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: [3.8, 3.9, '3.10']

    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v3
    - name: Set up Python ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      uses: actions/setup-python@v4
      with:
        python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
    - name: Install dependencies
      run: |
        python -m pip install --upgrade pip
        pip install -r requirements.txt
    - name: Run tests
      run: |
        pytest

docs.title: 2. Code Quality
docs.text:
name: Code Quality

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v3
    - name: Set up Python
      uses: actions/setup-python@v4
      with:
        python-version: '3.x'
    - name: Install dependencies
      run: |
        pip install ruff black
    - name: Run linters
      run: |
        ruff check .
        black --check .

docs.title: 3. Documentation Deployment
docs.text:
name: Deploy Docs

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v3
    - name: Set up Python
      uses: actions/setup-python@v4
      with:
        python-version: '3.x'
    - name: Install dependencies
      run: |
        pip install mkdocs-material
    - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
      run: |
        mkdocs gh-deploy --force

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Workflow Organization
  2. Keep workflows focused and modular
  3. Use reusable workflows

  4. Separate concerns (test, lint, deploy)

  5. Security

  6. Use secrets for sensitive data
  7. Limit permissions
  8. Use dependency caching

  9. Pin action versions

  10. Performance

  11. Use caching
  12. Run jobs in parallel
  13. Use matrix builds

  14. Optimize workflow steps

  15. Maintenance

  16. Document workflows
  17. Use consistent naming
  18. Keep workflows up to date
  19. Monitor workflow runs

docs.title: Useful Resources
docs.text:
• GitHub Actions Documentation
  • GitHub Actions Marketplace
  • GitHub Actions Examples

docs.title: What are GitHub Repository Templates?
docs.text: GitHub repository templates are pre-configured repositories that you can use as a starting point for new projects. They help maintain consistency across projects and save time by providing a standardized structure and configuration.

docs.title: Why Use Repository Templates?
docs.text:
• Consistency: Ensure all projects follow the same structure and best practices
  • Time Saving: Skip the initial setup and configuration
  • Best Practices: Enforce coding standards and project organization
  • Documentation: Include standard documentation templates
  • CI/CD: Pre-configured GitHub Actions workflows

docs.title: How to Use Repository Templates
docs.text:
1. Create a new repository
  2. Click "Use this template" button
  3. Choose a name for your new repository
  4. Start coding!

docs.title: Already have a repository?
docs.text:
• Option 1: Copy files manually, tedious but needs to be done only once.
  • Option 2: If you feel comfortable with the command line, you can try this solution.

docs.title: Creating Your Own Template
docs.text:
1. Create a repository with your desired structure
  2. Go to repository settings
  3. Enable "Template repository" option
  4. Share with your team!

docs.title: Best Practices for Templates
docs.text:
• Include a comprehensive README.md
  • Add a LICENSE file
  • Set up basic CI/CD workflows
  • Include common configuration files
  • Add documentation templates
  • Configure code quality tools

docs.title: What is MkDocs?
docs.text: MkDocs is a fast, simple, and static site generator designed specifically for building project documentation. It's written in Python and uses Markdown files to create beautiful documentation websites.

docs.title: Why Use MkDocs?
docs.text:
• Simple: Write documentation in Markdown
  • Fast: Quick build times
  • Beautiful: Multiple themes available
  • Search: Built-in search functionality
  • Versioning: Support for multiple versions
  • GitHub Pages: Easy deployment

docs.title: Getting Started with Material for MkDocs
Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use a consistent documentation structure
  • Include a search functionality
  • Add versioning for API documentation
  • Use relative links
  • Include a table of contents
  • Add code examples
  • Keep documentation up to date
  • Use a modern theme (like Material for MkDocs)

docs.title: What is pre-commit?
docs.text: pre-commit is a framework for managing and maintaining Git pre-commit hooks. It helps ensure code quality by running checks before each commit, preventing bad commits from entering your repository.

docs.title: Why Use pre-commit?
docs.text:
• Consistency: Enforce consistent code style across the project
  • Quality: Catch issues before they enter the repository
  • Automation: Automate common checks and fixes
  • Team Collaboration: Share the same checks across the team
  • Time Saving: Prevent issues early in the development process

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
# Using pip
pip install pre-commit

# Using uv
uv pip install pre-commit

docs.title: Basic Configuration
docs.text:
Create a .pre-commit-config.yaml file in your project root:

repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: trailing-whitespace
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: check-yaml
      - id: check-added-large-files
      - id: check-ast
      - id: check-json
      - id: check-merge-conflict
      - id: detect-private-key

  - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: v0.3.0
    hooks:
      - id: ruff
        args: [--fix]
      - id: ruff-format

docs.title: Installation in Repository
docs.text:
# Install the pre-commit hooks
pre-commit install

# Run against all files
pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
• ruff: Fast Python linter
  • black: Code formatter
  • isort: Import sorter

docs.title: Type Checking
docs.text:
• mypy: Static type checker
  • pyright: Type checker

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• bandit: Security linter
  • safety: Dependency checker
  • detect-secrets: Secrets detection

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• docformatter: Docstring formatter
  • pydocstyle: Docstring style checker

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Performance Impact

Running tests before every commit can be computationally expensive and slow down your workflow. Enable test hooks based on your team's needs and preferences.

  • pytest: Run tests
  • coverage: Check test coverage

docs.title: Hook Selection
docs.text:
• Choose hooks that match your project needs
  • Start with essential hooks and add more gradually
  • Consider performance impact

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
• Keep configurations in version control
  • Document any custom configurations
  • Use consistent settings across the team

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Use fast hooks for common checks
  • Configure hooks to run only on relevant files
  • Consider using cached results

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text:
• Keep hooks up to date
  • Review and update configurations regularly
  • Monitor hook performance

docs.title: Hook Failing
docs.text:
• Check hook documentation
  • Verify configuration
  • Run hook manually for debugging

docs.title: Performance Issues
docs.text:
• Use --verbose flag to identify slow hooks
  • Consider excluding certain files
  • Use caching where available

docs.title: False Positives
docs.text:
• Configure hooks to ignore specific patterns
  • Use inline comments to disable hooks
  • Adjust hook settings

docs.title: Additional Resources
docs.text:
• pre-commit Documentation
  • pre-commit-hooks Repository
  • Awesome pre-commit hooks

docs.title: What is pyproject.toml?
docs.text: pyproject.toml is a standardized configuration file for Python projects, introduced in PEP 517 and PEP 518. It's designed to replace multiple configuration files (like setup.py, setup.cfg, MANIFEST.in) with a single, unified configuration file.

docs.title: Why Use pyproject.toml?
docs.text:
• Standardization: One file to rule them all
  • Modern: Supports modern Python packaging tools
  • Readable: TOML format is more readable than INI or Python
  • Flexible: Can configure multiple tools in one place
  • Future-proof: The future of Python packaging

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"

[project]
name = "my-package"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "A sample package"
authors = [
    { name = "Your Name", email = "your.email@example.com" }
]
dependencies = [
    "requests>=2.28.0",
    "pandas>=1.5.0"
]

[tool.black]
line-length = 88
target-version = ['py38']

[tool.isort]
profile = "black"
multi_line_output = 3

docs.title: Common Tool Configurations
docs.text:
• ruff: Linting and formatting
  • isort: Import sorting
  • mypy: Type checking
  • pytest: Testing
  • coverage: Test coverage

docs.title: What is Ruff?
docs.text:
An extremely fast Python linter and code formatter, written in Rust.

The Ruff Linter is an extremely fast Python linter designed as a drop-in replacement for Flake8 (plus dozens of plugins), isort, pydocstyle, pyupgrade, autoflake, and more.

The Ruff formatter is an extremely fast Python code formatter designed as a drop-in replacement for Black, available as part of the ruff CLI via ruff format.

What does uv stand for?

Not sure, but my guess would be "RUst Fast Formatter". Some other creative names are

docs.title: Why is Ruff Cool?
docs.text:
• Speed: Up to 10-100x faster than traditional linters
  • Comprehensive: Implements 500+ rules from popular linters
  • Modern: Written in Rust for performance
  • Configurable: Highly customizable rule sets
  • Compatible: Works with existing tool configurations

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
# Using pip
pip install ruff

# Using uv
uv pip install ruff

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
# Lint your code
ruff check .

# Fix automatically fixable issues
ruff check --fix .

# Show available rules
ruff rule list

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Add to your pyproject.toml:

[tool.ruff]
# Enable all rules
select = ["E", "F", "B", "I"]

# Ignore specific rules
ignore = ["E501"]

# Line length
line-length = 88

# Target Python version
target-version = "py38"

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Fast: Parallel processing of files
  • Comprehensive: Implements rules from:
  • flake8
  • isort
  • pyupgrade
  • autoflake
  • and many more
  • Configurable: Fine-grained control over rules
  • IDE Integration: Works with VS Code, PyCharm, etc.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: For more information, visit: - Ruff Documentation - GitHub Repository

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Start with a basic rule set and gradually add more
  • Use auto-fix where possible
  • Configure rules to match your team's style guide
  • Integrate with your IDE for real-time feedback
  • Use in CI/CD pipelines for consistent code quality

docs.title: What is pytest?
docs.text: pytest is a powerful, flexible, and easy-to-use testing framework for Python. It's designed to make testing simple and scalable, with features that make it easy to write and maintain tests.

docs.title: Why Use pytest?
docs.text:
• Simple: Easy to get started
  • Powerful: Rich ecosystem of plugins
  • Flexible: Multiple ways to organize tests
  • Informative: Detailed failure reports
  • Extensible: Large plugin ecosystem

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
Note

Notice that the tests directory is outside of the my_package directory. This is a best practice since we do not want to pollute the my_package directory with test files. Also, the folder structure should mirror the source code structure.

my_project/
├── my_package/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── main.py
│   ├── users/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── models.py
│   │   └── services.py
│   └── orders/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── models.py
│       └── services.py
├── tests/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── conftest.py
│   ├── test_main.py
│   ├── users/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── test_models.py
│   │   └── test_services.py
│   └── orders/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── test_models.py
│       └── test_services.py
├── pyproject.toml
└── README.md

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install pytest
# or
uv pip install pytest

docs.title: Basic Test Example
docs.text:
# tests/test_main.py
def test_addition():
    assert 1 + 1 == 2

def test_string_operations():
    assert "hello" + " world" == "hello world"

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
# Run all tests
pytest

# Run specific test file
pytest tests/test_main.py

# Run specific test
pytest tests/test_main.py::test_addition

# Run with verbose output
pytest -v

# Run with print statements
pytest -s

docs.title: Fixtures
docs.text:
# tests/conftest.py
import pytest

@pytest.fixture
def sample_data():
    return {"key": "value"}

# tests/test_main.py
def test_with_fixture(sample_data):
    assert sample_data["key"] == "value"

docs.title: Parametrize Tests
docs.text:
@pytest.mark.parametrize("input,expected", [
    (1, 2),
    (2, 4),
    (3, 6),
])
def test_double(input, expected):
    assert input * 2 == expected

docs.title: Markers
docs.text:
@pytest.mark.slow
def test_slow_operation():
    # This test will be skipped unless explicitly run
    pass

# Run only slow tests
pytest -m slow

docs.title: Test Organization
docs.text:
• Keep tests in a separate tests directory
  • Mirror your source code structure
  • Use conftest.py for shared fixtures

docs.title: Test Naming
docs.text:
• Use descriptive test names
  • Follow the pattern test_<function>_<scenario>
  • Make test names readable

docs.title: Test Structure
docs.text:
• Arrange-Act-Assert pattern
  • One assertion per test
  • Keep tests independent

docs.title: Fixtures
docs.text:
• Use fixtures for setup and teardown
  • Keep fixtures focused and reusable
  • Use conftest.py for shared fixtures

docs.title: Test Coverage
docs.text:
• Aim for high coverage
  • Focus on critical paths
  • Don't test implementation details

docs.title: Common pytest Plugins
docs.text:
• pytest-cov: Coverage reporting
  • pytest-xdist: Parallel test execution
  • pytest-mock: Mocking support
  • pytest-asyncio: Async test support
  • pytest-bdd: Behavior-driven development

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Add to your pyproject.toml:

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
python_files = ["test_*.py"]
addopts = "-v --cov=src"

docs.title: uv: The Fast Python Package Manager
What is uv?
docs.text:
An extremely fast Python package and project manager, written in Rust.

It's developed by Astral and is designed to be a drop-in replacement for pip with significant performance improvements. Find the documentation here.

What does uv stand for?

Nothing in specific, here's a comment from the uv repo:

uv was given to us on PyPI, is Astral-themed (i.e. ultraviolet or universal), and is short and easy to type.

docs.title: Why is uv Cool?
docs.text:
• Speed: Up to 10-100x faster than pip
  • Reliability: Better dependency resolution
  • Compatibility: Works with existing requirements.txt and pyproject.toml
  • Modern: Written in Rust for performance
  • Simple: Familiar interface for pip users

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
# Using curl
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Using pip
pip install uv

docs.title: Basic Commands
docs.text:
# Install packages
uv pip install pandas numpy

# Install from requirements.txt
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

# Create a virtual environment
uv venv

# Install in editable mode
uv pip install -e .

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Fast Installation: Parallel downloads and installations
  • Smart Caching: Efficient package caching
  • Virtual Environments: Built-in venv support
  • Wheel Building: Fast wheel building for local packages
  • Lock Files: Generate and use lock files for reproducible installations

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: For more information, visit the official documentation: - uv Documentation - GitHub Repository

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use lock files for reproducible environments
  • Leverage the cache for faster installations
  • Use virtual environments for project isolation
  • Consider using uv in CI/CD pipelines for faster builds

docs.title: Recommended Settings
docs.text: Here are our recommended VS Code settings for Python development. These settings are optimized for our development workflow and integrate well with the tools we use.

docs.title: Python Development Settings
docs.text:
{
    "[python]": {
        "editor.defaultFormatter": "charliermarsh.ruff",
        "editor.formatOnSave": true,
        "editor.codeActionsOnSave": {
            "source.organizeImports": "explicit"
        }
    }
}

These settings: - Use Ruff as the default formatter for Python files - Enable format on save - Configure import organization to run explicitly

docs.title: Testing Configuration
docs.text:
{
    "python.testing.pytestArgs": [
        "tests"
    ],
    "python.testing.unittestEnabled": false,
    "python.testing.pytestEnabled": true
}

This configuration: - Sets up pytest as the default test runner - Configures the tests directory - Disables unittest in favor of pytest

docs.title: Code Quality Tools
docs.text:
{
    "sonarlint.connectedMode.project": {
        "connectionId": "sea-ai",
        "projectKey": "SEA-AI_seavision"
    },
    "pylint.ignorePatterns": [
        "*test*.py"
    ]
}

These settings: - Configure SonarLint for code quality analysis - Set up Pylint to ignore test files

docs.title: File Management
docs.text:
{
    "files.exclude": {
        "**/.mypy_cache": true,
        "**/.pytest_cache": true,
        "**/.ruff_cache": true
    }
}

This helps keep your workspace clean by hiding: - Type checking cache files - Test cache directories - Linter cache files

docs.title: Additional Features
docs.text:
{
    "emojisense.languages": {
        "python": true
    }
}

Enables emoji support in Python files.

docs.title: Recommended Extensions

1. Python (Microsoft)
  2. Python language support
  3. IntelliSense
  4. Linting

  17. Best practices enforcement

  18. GitLens (GitKraken)

  19. Enhanced Git capabilities
  20. Blame annotations

  21. History visualization

  22. Markdown All in One (Yu Zhang)

  23. Markdown support
  24. Preview
  25. Auto-completion

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Workspace Settings
  2. Use workspace settings for project-specific configurations
  3. Share settings with team members

  4. Document any custom settings

  5. Extension Management

  6. Keep extensions up to date
  7. Remove unused extensions

  8. Use recommended extensions list

  9. Keyboard Shortcuts

  10. Learn common shortcuts
  11. Customize for your workflow

  12. Use command palette (Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + P)

  13. Integrated Terminal

  14. Use the integrated terminal
  15. Configure your preferred shell
  16. Use multiple terminals when needed

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Common issues and solutions:

  1. Formatting Not Working
  2. Check if Ruff is installed
  3. Verify extension is enabled

  4. Check file associations

  5. Linting Issues

  6. Clear cache files
  7. Restart VS Code

  8. Check extension settings

  9. Performance Problems

  10. Disable unnecessary extensions
  11. Clear workspace storage
  12. Check file watchers

This module demonstrates the use of dataclasses in Python.

A nested address dataclass example.

Profile

A more complex profile dataclass with nested dataclass.

A simple user dataclass example.

Create a sample profile with nested dataclasses.

create_sample_user

Create a sample user for demonstration.

Address(
    street: str, city: str, country: str, postal_code: str
)

A nested address dataclass example.

Street address including house number

TYPE: str

city

City name

TYPE: str

country

Country name

TYPE: str

Postal code

TYPE: str

A more complex profile dataclass with nested dataclass.

List of users following this profile

docs.title: templatepy.demo.pydantic_demo
docs.text:
This module demonstrates the use of pydantic in Python.

CLASS DESCRIPTION Address

Address model with validation.

Profile

Profile model with nested models.

User

Complete user model with additional fields.

UserBase

Base user model with common attributes.

UserCreate

Model for creating a new user.

FUNCTION DESCRIPTION create_sample_profile

Create a sample profile with nested models.

create_sample_user

Create a sample user for demonstration.

docs.title: templatepy.demo.pydantic_demo.Address pydantic-model
docs.text:
Bases: BaseModel

Address model with validation.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION street

Street address including house number

TYPE: str

city

City name

TYPE: str

country

Country name

TYPE: str

postal_code

US postal code in format: 12345 or 12345-6789

TYPE: str

Fields:

  • street (str)
  • city (str)
  • country (str)
  • postal_code (str)

docs.title: city pydantic-field
docs.text:
city: str

City name

docs.title: country pydantic-field
docs.text:
country: str

Country name

docs.title: postal_code pydantic-field
docs.text:
postal_code: str

US postal code in format: 12345 or 12345-6789

docs.title: street pydantic-field
docs.text:
street: str

Street address including house number

docs.title: templatepy.demo.pydantic_demo.Profile pydantic-model
docs.text:
Bases: BaseModel

Profile model with nested models.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION user

User information

TYPE: User

address

User's address details

TYPE: Address | None DEFAULT: None

bio

User's biography - maximum 500 characters

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

followers

List of users following this profile

TYPE: List[User] DEFAULT: <dynamic>

Fields:

  • user (User)
  • address (Optional[Address])
  • bio (Optional[str])
  • followers (List[User])

docs.title: address pydantic-field
docs.text:
address: Optional[Address] = None

User's address details

docs.title: bio pydantic-field
docs.text:
bio: Optional[str] = None

User's biography - maximum 500 characters

docs.title: followers pydantic-field
docs.text:
followers: List[User]

List of users following this profile

docs.title: user pydantic-field
docs.text:
user: User

User information

docs.title: templatepy.demo.pydantic_demo.User pydantic-model
docs.text:
Bases: UserBase

Complete user model with additional fields.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION name

User's full name - must contain a space

TYPE: str

email

User's email address

TYPE: str

age

User's age - must be between 0 and 120

TYPE: int

is_active

Whether the user account is active

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

id

Unique identifier for the user

TYPE: int

created_at

Timestamp when the user was created

TYPE: datetime DEFAULT: <dynamic>

tags

List of tags associated with the user

TYPE: List[str] DEFAULT: <dynamic>

website

User's personal website URL

TYPE: HttpUrl | None DEFAULT: None

Fields:

  • name (str)
  • email (str)
  • age (int)
  • is_active (bool)
  • id (int)
  • created_at (datetime)
  • tags (List[str])
  • website (Optional[HttpUrl])

Validators:

  • name_must_contain_space → name

docs.title: created_at pydantic-field
docs.text:
created_at: datetime

Timestamp when the user was created

docs.title: id pydantic-field
docs.text:
id: int

Unique identifier for the user

docs.title: tags pydantic-field
docs.text:
tags: List[str]

List of tags associated with the user

docs.title: website pydantic-field
docs.text:
website: Optional[HttpUrl] = None

User's personal website URL

docs.title: name_must_contain_space pydantic-validator
docs.text:
name_must_contain_space(v: str) -> str

Validate that the name contains a space.

docs.title: templatepy.demo.pydantic_demo.UserCreate pydantic-model
docs.text:
Bases: UserBase

Model for creating a new user.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION name

User's full name - must contain a space

TYPE: str

email

User's email address

TYPE: str

age

User's age - must be between 0 and 120

TYPE: int

is_active

Whether the user account is active

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

password

User's password - minimum 8 characters

TYPE: str

Fields:

  • name (str)
  • email (str)
  • age (int)
  • is_active (bool)
  • password (str)

docs.text:
password: str

User's password - minimum 8 characters
